html:
The global landscape is largely shaped today by efforts to secure a peace deal between the US and Iran amidst ongoing technical talks in Doha, following a war on Iran that has fundamentally reshaped the global energy sector and impacted the Strait of Hormuz.

US-Iran Diplomacy: Indirect technical talks are underway in Doha between US and Iranian officials, aiming to secure a peace deal and restart shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. While working groups have been established to negotiate a final agreement, Iran has reportedly suspended negotiations, demanding that the Hormuz issue and the liberation of Lebanon be addressed first. A former US Envoy suggests that releasing frozen Iranian funds is politically difficult for Trump, whose administration is giving diplomacy an additional opportunity after Iran suspended talks. Separately, US-Saudi relations are strained following a dispute over Saudi Arabia's initial refusal to allow Washington to use its bases for an Iran operation, while Iran warns of an immediate response to any threat, citing a US commitment to restrain Israel and reiterating that its missile and drone capabilities are non-negotiable.

European Security & Economy: Ukraine's extended campaign against Russian energy infrastructure is disrupting fuel supplies and military logistics, leading to mounting shortages in occupied Crimea and raising questions about pressure on Putin towards negotiations. In wider Europe, the EU is preparing a digital euro to reduce dependence on major payment systems, and Ireland has taken the presidency of the EU, with Zelenskyy urging the bloc to keep promises on Ukraine accession talks. Ahead of the upcoming NATO summit in Ankara, Turkey has arrested over 200 activists, journalists, and human rights advocates amid a strict public gatherings ban.

Global Security & Conflicts: Amnesty International accuses Sudan's Rapid Support Forces (RSF) of committing crimes against humanity and ethnic cleansing in el-Fasher, with fears now rising that El Obeid could see an "El Fasher repeat" amid RSF drone strikes. A cholera outbreak in Sudan has also resulted in 120 deaths. Meanwhile, in the Middle East, Israeli operations continue, with Netanyahu stating Israel will remain in Lebanon, Syria, and Gaza "indefinitely" and establishing new checkpoints near Naqoura. Lebanese health ministry figures report 4,297 deaths from Israeli attacks since March 2, and Israeli forces continue arrests in the occupied West Bank and strikes in Gaza. Afghanistan's drone strikes into Pakistan have also severely damaged relations between the two nations, perpetuating a cycle of cross-border attacks.

Economic & Tech Trends: The "Iran war" has supercharged the pivot to renewable energy and electric vehicles worldwide, with high oil and gas prices making these technologies financially attractive and enhancing energy security. However, global ocean surface temperatures have hit a record high for June, pushing the world into "uncharted territory." In Australia, rapid demand for AI datacenters could stoke inflation and crowd out land for housing, leading to calls for a halt on new approvals. On the technology front, the US has lifted an export ban on Anthropic's advanced AI tools, Fable and Mythos, which were previously suspended over hacking concerns.

US Domestic & Social Issues: In the United States, the Supreme Court upheld birthright citizenship, marking a major setback for Trump's immigration agenda. The ruling comes as Trump made more than $1 billion from crypto in his first year back in office, an income he attributes to "blind accounts." Separately, a rebel group of ultra-conservative Catholics, the Society of Saint Pius X, defied Pope Leo by ordaining bishops without his consent, resulting in automatic excommunication and posing a crisis for Pope Leo.
